sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Antepartum haemorrhage is bleeding from the genital tract after approximately 20 weeks gestation and before birth. Important causes include placenta praevia, placental abruption, labour-related bleeding, uterine rupture, and vasa praevia, and any significant bleeding should be treated as a time-critical obstetric emergency.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Vaginal bleeding, which may be light, heavy, intermittent, or partly concealed
- Abdominal pain, uterine tenderness, or a rigid abdomen
- Back pain, contractions, or signs of labour
- Syncope, tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, or shock
- Reduced fetal movement if this is known or reported

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Pregnant patients may compensate well before obvious shock develops and can deteriorate rapidly
- Placenta praevia may bleed with little pain, while placental abruption often causes pain, uterine irritability, or tenderness
- Significant haemorrhage may be partly concealed and visible blood loss can underestimate severity
- Do not perform digital vaginal examination in the prehospital setting
- Suspected placenta praevia or placental abruption should be treated as high risk even if the bleeding is minor or has settled

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient to minimise aortocaval compression if late pregnancy is suspected
- Keep the patient warm, reassured, and under close observation
- Perform frequent vital signs and reassessment
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Treat any associated labour, pain, or collapse as high risk
- Prioritise urgent transport and early pre-notification to an appropriate obstetric-capable facility, even if the bleeding appears to have stopped

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access early if bleeding is significant, the patient is unstable, or deterioration is possible
- Begin resuscitative fluid therapy where clinically indicated while expediting transfer
- Consider analgesia and anti-emetic therapy within scope and protocol
- Consider point-of-care haemoglobin or other monitoring only if this does not delay transfer
- Escalate early to obstetric, retrieval, or senior clinical support

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Any substantial bleeding, pain, syncope, or abnormal observations should be treated seriously even if bleeding has slowed or stopped
- Placental abruption and uterine rupture can present with pain and shock out of proportion to visible blood loss
- Record gestation if known, bleeding description, pain, and whether fetal movement was reported
